speaker: interviewer
text: June 2, 1996 interview with Rabbi Herman Schaalman. Name at birth was the same with an extra N added at the end of the name. Interviewer, Doris Lazarus-- the interview is being conducted in Chicago, Illinois, USA, in English. June 2, 1996-- I'm Doris Lazarus conducting an interview with Rabbi Herman Schaalman in his home, Chicago, Illinois. Please begin by telling us your full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Herman Ezra Schaalman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you please spell the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's S-C-H-A-A-L-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the name that you were given at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, except, it was spelled a little differently. Herman had two N's then and Schaalman had two N's.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned a middle name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ezra.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that also a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a Hebrew name. And it was after a grandfather that had passed away by the time I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birthday, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: April 28th, 1916.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you are how old today?

speaker: interviewee
text: 80 and a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Munich, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about Munich the way it was before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, basically, of course, Munich was the cultural and so we call it maybe perhaps even intellectual hub of Germany at the time. It had lots of artists and theaters. I think we had four opera houses that were operating at the same time, one devoted, for instance, entirely to Wagner, et cetera, et cetera. And there was a great university, fine library, one of the great libraries, which incidentally contained one of the very few complete manuscripts of the Babylonian Talmud. So Munich was a considerable city, as I recall it, both during the times that I lived there and in retrospect.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about from a Jewish perspective? Could you describe the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was about 10,000 Jews living in, in Munich in the '20s after the-- between the two world wars. There was a, a, an orthodox community. But the main community was what was known as German liberal, which is closer to what in America would be conservative Judaism. There was a very large synagogue that been built in the 1870s, if I remember right. And to the best of my recollection, it seated close to 3,000 people and was really a major edifice down the center of the city, even though it was on a side street. Incidentally, it was destroyed at the specific instruction of Adolf Hitler because he belonged to a, an artists' club that was right across the street. And he couldn't abide the sight of the synagogue when he looked out the window. So even as I-- if I remember right and if I have the story correct, before Kristallnacht, that synagogue already had been slated to destruction. Well, we lived in an apartment building. We happened to have the top floor, which was the smallest. The apartments-- there was just one apartment per floor. These were large apartments. And most of the people in the apartment building actually were Jewish at the time that I remember, my childhood. I once, when I returned to Germany with my grandchildren, revisit my home. And when I looked at the, the names on, on the outside device that holds these names, I couldn't recognize any except the superintendent. It was the same man.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I rang his bell. And he came up from the basement. And he took one look. He immediately recognized me. And then when I asked him whether there was any possibility of going back to our own apartment so I could show my grandchildren what it was like, he says, no, you don't want to. The whole house is full of Nazis, said he. This was in the, well, early '90s. Just recently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fairly recently, maybe late '80s, maybe late '80s or early '90s. Now he had happened to have been a radical socialist. And what to him was a Nazi may not have really been a Nazi. But it indicated to him that I would be most unwelcome, shall we say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if your father had been born in Munich and if his ancestors had also lived in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was born in a small town called [INAUDIBLE] in Franconia, which is part of Bavaria. And a friend of his who was a Jewish historian by the name of Strauss once called his attention to the fact that he had traced our family back to 1519 when, according to the records, the rich Jew Schaalman was driven out of a town called Regensburg, which is still in existence to this day. It's maybe a larger town in Franconia than any other. And my guess is that if he had lived there and was already considered to be wealthy at the time that it is not unlikely that his ancestors would have lived either in the city or around that part for a hundred of 200 years earlier. My father's birthplace is not far from Regensburg so it is likely, even though we don't have necessarily all the intermediate kinds of links that the family was primarily found in that part of Bavarian and southern Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your mother's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was born in Ukraine and was brought to Germany when she was six months old because her family was fleeing a pogrom in Ukraine. And that must have been in 19-- in 1893, because that's when she was born. And it was towards the end of that year that she was taken to Nuremberg, where the family then resided. And she met my father I think at university, but I am not really sure. That may have been her brother or brothers that introduced her to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was my two younger brothers. I'm the oldest of the three. And Ernst is five years younger. And Manfred is eight years younger than I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a mathematician and physicist and taught in, in Munich in the kind of high school that was devoted to the sciences. And therefore, they had a program of education that far exceeded what let's say high school students would, would cover in, in the, m contemporary Chicago. So they, they went deep into all kinds of mathematical problems already way beyond calculus and so on. And he taught until 1936, when he was unwillingly put into pension because at that time, the Nazis had long already determined that Jewish teachers could no longer teach in German schools. The reason he lasted that long was that he had been a decorated veteran of the First World War and as a veteran had been given certainly what you might call a protective privilege until 1936. Then he was pensioned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe your father as a religious man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so-- we had a very observant home. There was no question about kashrut. There was simply not even a question. We never ate in a restaurant that I can recall. We observed. We, we prayed daily together, my father, my brothers and I, especially after my having become a bar mitzvah. My father and I, they'd fill in the morning. And we doesn't gather each morning. Shabbos was kept very scrupulously. I, for instance, decided after my bar mitzvah that I would no longer write on the Sabbath. And therefore, my father petitioned my high school to relieve me from attending classes on the Sabbath. Because school was normally from Monday through Saturday in those days. And I was granted the opportunity to be absent because I was a fairly good student and they were quite sure that I could make up whatever I missed. And of course, both Friday evening and Saturday morning, regularly without any fail, no matter where we were or what circumstances, we were in synagogue. So yes, we were a very observant home. Obviously, we would observe every holiday and fast day and so on. And it, it was a joyously Jewish home. My parents both understood somehow to, to make Jewish observance exciting and joys and beautiful. My father had a lovely voice. My mother always collaborated in making sure that the, that the food and the, the appointments of the table were just tip-top. So the house really had a shine of, of Jewishness about it. And I've tried to convey this in a different way, because I'm now a reform rabbi, to my own household, my own children and grandchildren. And I think they, too, know something about-- certainly, our children do-- of the joy of being an observant Jew in a different mode than the one that I was used to from my home.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your father had a beautiful singing voice. Did he ever sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. First of all, he had friends who would come and play the piano. And they would have, well, soirees in the house. He loved to sing, especially some stuff from Wagner. My father was peculiarly a, an aficionado of, of Wagner operas. But he also knew all of the proper melodic treatments of every one of the, the prayers for every day for Sabbath or holidays, all of the variations. He had been trained by his own father who had been a, a teacher of, of Hebrew. And I learned a lot, too, because my father used to rehearse at home. And I heard the melodies. And then there was an orphanage about a half hour's walk away from our home. And another man and he undertook to conduct services there every Friday night for the inmates of that orphanage and for some [INAUDIBLE] him who lived nearby who would come and constitute an adult minyan. And it was their then, too, that from time to time, especially after I had become a bar mitzvah, I was given the opportunity to conduct services, to chant them as well. And my own love of the Nusach, as it is called and of whatever knowledge I gathered there of liturgy comes from the exposure there. In fact, twice-- and this was really unusual-- and I had a rather pleasant, fairly high soprano voice, I suppose.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was given the opportunity at family services in the big synagogue to be the chazzan for the entire community then. That was twice on, on Hanukkah, if I remember correctly. So that I-- that was with the organ and a, and a big choir. And I was the boy chazzan, you know? [LAUGHS] I had almost forgotten about that. But that's a long time ago. You mentioned your bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you trained with your Jewish education?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had several cantors in this big congregation-- in fact, usually three. And one of them, a cantor [? Lachman ?] who lived near our house undertook, at my father's behest, to train me. And I was only the second person in the history of that congregation who chanted the entire Sidrah, and of course, the Haftorah then afterwards, and called up all of the people that got aliyot during that time. I must tell you that the fright and excitement of that event is still in my knees whenever I step into the pulpit. I can still feel how anxious I was. But it went very, very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was almost 70 years ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's not, not far from that-- 67 for sure by now, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember if there was a celebration following your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, my-- the whole family, my mother's family. My father had three brothers and two sisters. They came with their family. It was in our apartment. I, I still remember the-- that the bathtub was filled with water to accommodate fish that were kept alive until the cook, on Friday afternoon prepared for the meal. Oh, there must have been 50, 60 people for, for dinner at our-- I remembered it so well because my parents gave up their bedroom. The Furniture was totally taken out. And temporary dining tables were set up so that the whole family could eat together in one room. And it, it was really made festive for the-- somewhere, I have a photograph of, of that moment. And of course, when occasionally I look at it and realize that with the exception of my two brothers, everyone on the photo is, to the best of my knowledge, gone by now. And then, you know, it brings back roots and memories that are very precious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what age you were when you decided that you wanted to become a rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: There had been some talk. My-- in my mother's family, there was a tradition-- whether it is true or not, I don't know-- that there had been rabbis in the past in Ukraine and, and Eastern Europe. And I knew that my grandfather on my father's side, whom I also didn't know he was dead before I was born, had been a Melamed, a Hebrew teacher. And so there was a, a natural bend in that direction. And then, I must say to you, that my becoming a bar mitzvah was really a decisive moment in my life. And especially in retrospect, it was possibly what you might call a defining moment. And I decided then that that's what I wanted to be. There were, during my growing up, my teenage years, a couple of times when I wavered. But I always came back to it and pursued it. And but-- when I was then a graduate from high school at age 19, I enrolled in [INAUDIBLE], the liberal rabbinic seminary in Berlin, and arrived there on May 1st, 1935, and began my rabbinic studies at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of education did you receive before you entered your rabbinical studies?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, you had a choice of three different types of high schools. And mind you, they were all nine schools. You went in. You made that decision. Or that decision was really made for you, because when you were nine years old, you didn't really know what your life should be like. And in my instance, my parents decided that I should go to what was known as the humanist high school. It was a high school that had an emphasis on ancient languages-- nine years of Latin, six years of Greek, just to-- just for starters. I had only one modern language. And that was an elective. And I took a couple or three three years of, of English at the end of my high school life. But we went to school from 8:00 in the morning till 12:40 and sometimes still 1:20. And if you took any electives, they were in the afternoon, such as music, or language, or whatever. So it was the typical middle European education. And well, it has stood me in good stead in many ways. I had, for instance, seven years of history, and I think nine years of geography. I don't even remember it all. It's a long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: there any other Jewish students in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the best of my knowledge, there was one other in my class that I know for sure. And I don't think there was any other. The high school I went to was, because it was humanist and also located in a, in a part of the city where there were some rather lovely homes, and so there were aristocrats and children of high military officers and so on that were among the people that were in the school. But you see, you need to understand that that school had maybe 500, 600 students. And we-- if you divide it up over nine years, you've got about 50, 60 per year. And then we were divided up into two sections. I happened to be, I think, in the B section. I don't remember that anymore. But there were roughly 30 fellow students. With the other section in the same year, we had almost no contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's really what-- there were no girls, of course. This was Bavaria. This was only a boys' school. And where there were girls we knew, we had to know from entirely different settings. Did you belong to any organizations during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a Jewish Boy Scout, and also for a while, a member of a Zionist youth movement called the Kameraden, many of whom made aliyah and founded kibbutzim in, in Israel, in-- well, first in Palestine and later in Israel. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go any further, I'd like to talk about your mother a little bit. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Regina, Regina, nicknamed Gina-- a very bright, lovable person. She was one of the very few women students who attended university already in the late teens, right after the war, and early '20s. She never finished, simply because the household duties eventually became too much.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I remember when I must have been three or four years old, going with my mother to university, she leaving me at a fountain-- I can still see it. Why do I see it? Because a big, black dog came and scared the hell out of me. And it-- if it hadn't been for a man who came by who called the dog off or chased him away, I don't know. I would have died of fright. So that was just about then the end. Because Mother then realized that unless she had help, which then eventually we a maid, a live-in maid soon thereafter. But also, not long after that, my second brother arrived. And that kind of ended my mother's academic career.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it is indicative of the fact that she came from an intellectual family, an Eastern European family. Both her brothers were physicians, one a surgeon and the other one an ophthalmologist. And she, she was a very competent lady. Among her really notable achievements was the fact that despite the fact that she was really Eastern European, and in Germany there was a tremendous conflict, you might almost say, between the true German Jews and those who had eastern descent or origins.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you explain that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the German Jews were really not secure enough not to be scared by the influx of Eastern European Jewry who spoke Yiddish, which is distorted German, who had these peculiar, quaint customs, and who didn't quite dress like everybody else, and who simply were the poor relatives. And German Jews had just clawed their way into some form of, of equality. It was, I think, illusory to begin with. But not everybody realized that. In fact, they fought hard to become recognized and accepted citizens of, of Germany. And this, they felt-- perhaps not unjustifiably, actually-- that Eastern European Jews were a threat to their very fragile standing. Because, of course, the anti-Semites made fun of the Eastern European Jews or Yiddish, and their behavior, they're-- they were, many of them were bearded, et cetera. And my father married this Eastern European Jewess on the day that he was called to the, to the army. They gave-- he was in uniform. They gave him two hours I understand, of course, I wasn't there-- understand my mother fainted under the chuppah. And she didn't see my father for quite a while after, till he had a furlough, during which they produced me. Because I was born in the middle of the war, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I can tell you, for instance, that my father's German family, during the four and 1/2 years that he was on the front, never wrote him, never sent him a package, because they were so outraged that he had tainted the family's reputation by marrying someone who was not a German Jewess. My mother had the satisfaction that she was the only one in this rather large family that had three sons. So all the bar mitzvahs happened in Munich. And everybody came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And over the years, she was and irresistibly charming lady. And she managed to convince the rest of my father's Germanic family that she was a worthy member. In fact, as I say, she helped produce those sons that became the, the next generation, at least in that regard.

speaker: interviewee
text: So mother was an accomplished person. And despite the fact that she was of Eastern European descent, she became the president of the Women's Division of B'nai B'rith in Munich. And you must understand that B'nai B'rith in Europe altogether, but surely be in Germany, was the top Jewish organization with the greatest prestige. If you belong to B'nai B'rith, you was somebody. You had arrived. I I, would perhaps say here in Chicago, it was like maybe belonging to the Standard Club or something like that in earlier days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that my mother became the president of that most prestigious woman organization was enormous. And then breaking total, totally with presidents, they even re-elected for another term, which had never happened before. So you can tell that my mother had the wherewithal to establish herself to be a person and personage in her own right. She lived till she was 93. She only died 10 years ago. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you look back at that period in Germany when you were growing up, do you remember any anti-Semitism directed towards you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were certainly incidents. But the first exposure really to the Nazis, which is really, in a way, the, shall we say, the main evidence of all of this, happened when the Putsch occurred in Munich. That was the first time that the Nazis tried to seize power. And it was, I think, in 1923. And I remember that on that day, the maid had to take me to public school, and that we passed as [INAUDIBLE], I believe-- that's at least my memory-- with Nazi flags. And I was given the feeling that this was menacing. This was threatening. And that was the, the earliest that they can remember.
